paper_title: Glacier Length Change and Climate Reconstruction

methods.description: Describes the process of collecting and compiling historical and recent glacier length change data for Alpine glaciers, using various sources like historical records, photographs, dendrochronology, carbon-14 dating, and modern monitoring networks.

steps.description: Collect historical glacier length change data from sources like photographs, paintings, written records, and tree positions.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect historical glacier length change data from diverse sources
inputs: Tree samples near historical glacier extents

steps.description: Date historical records using dendrochronology or carbon-14 methods where applicable.
steps.searchable_summary: Date historical glacier extent data using dendrochronology or carbon-14 dating
outputs: Dated historical glacier extent/length data points

steps.description: Compile historical data for specific glaciers (Grosser Aletsch, Gorner, Rhone, Rosen-laui, Unterer Grindelwald, Mer de Glace, Bossons).
steps.searchable_summary: Compile historical glacier length data for specific Alpine glaciers
inputs: Dated historical glacier extent/length data points
outputs: Compiled historical glacier length time series for individual glaciers

steps.description: Obtain recent glacier length change data from monitoring networks (e.g., Swiss Glacier Monitoring Network) and other sources.
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain recent glacier length change data from monitoring networks
inputs: Access to glacier monitoring network databases
outputs: Recent glacier length time series data

steps.description: Combine historical and recent data series for analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: Combine historical and recent glacier length time series data
inputs: Compiled historical glacier length time series
outputs: Long-term, combined glacier length change time series for individual glaciers

methods.description: Implementation and application of a macroscopic dynamical system model (LV-model) to represent glacier dynamics, specifically the transfer function from volume change to length change, driven by changes in the equilibrium line elevation (ELA).

steps.description: Define the LV-model dynamical system equations for glacier length (L) and volume (V) based on Eq. 40 in Lüthi (2009).
steps.searchable_summary: Define the LV-model dynamical system equations for glacier length and volume
outputs: Model equations defined in code/mathematical framework

steps.description: Define model parameters including vertical gradient of local mass balance rate (ġ), bed slope (s), relaxation time constant (⌧ a), and volume-length scaling parameters (µ, a).
inputs: Parameter values or methods to derive them (ġ, s, ⌧ a, µ=7/5, a)

steps.description: Implement the volume-length scaling relation V = a L µ with µ = 7/5, updating parameter 'a' based on ġ, s, and current length L (Lüthi, 2009, Eqs. 21, 37, 38).
steps.searchable_summary: Implement volume-length scaling relation and update parameter 'a'

steps.description: Define the model forcing term Z(t) = z 0 z ELA (t), where z 0 is the highest bed point and z ELA (t) is the time-dependent equilibrium line elevation.
steps.searchable_summary: Define the model forcing based on ELA history
inputs: Time series of Equilibrium Line Altitude (z ELA (t))

steps.description: Integrate the dynamical system equations over time, using the defined forcing Z(t), to simulate glacier length and volume changes.
steps.searchable_summary: Integrate the LV-model dynamical system equations over time
outputs: Simulated time series of glacier length (L(t))
Simulated time series of glacier volume (V(t))

methods.method_name: Comparison of Reconstructed ELA with Instrumental Data
methods.description: Process for comparing the reconstructed Equilibrium Line Altitude (ELA) history, derived from glacier length change modeling, with independent instrumental climate records (temperature and precipitation) to validate the reconstruction and evaluate climate forcing.

steps.description: Obtain instrumental summer temperature data from meteorological stations (e.g., Grand Saint Bernard) and multi-station reconstructions (e.g., HISTALP CRSM Alpin, Casty et al.).
inputs: Data sources (Begert et al., 2005; Auer et al., 2007; Casty et al., 2005)

steps.description: Obtain instrumental winter and spring precipitation data from gridded reconstructions (e.g., Pauling et al.).
steps.searchable_summary: Obtain instrumental winter and spring precipitation data
inputs: Grid cell information (12 grid cells over Alpine area)
outputs: Instrumental winter and spring precipitation time series

steps.description: Calculate running means (e.g., 5-year running means) for instrumental temperature and precipitation records for smoothing.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate running means for climate time series
inputs: Window size for running mean (e.g., 5 years)

steps.description: Compare the reconstructed ELA history with the smoothed instrumental summer temperature records.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare reconstructed ELA time series with smoothed temperature time series
inputs: Reconstructed ELA time series (output from modeling)
Smoothed instrumental summer temperature time series

steps.description: Determine a sensitivity factor (f s = 80-100 m K 1) between reconstructed ELA and temperature to match variability.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate sensitivity factor between ELA and temperature
inputs: Smoothed instrumental summer temperature time series

steps.description: Compare the reconstructed ELA history with the smoothed instrumental winter and spring precipitation records.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare reconstructed ELA time series with smoothed precipitation time series
inputs: Smoothed instrumental winter and spring precipitation time series

steps.description: Analyze the timing and magnitude of variations in reconstructed ELA and instrumental records to identify agreements and discrepancies.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze agreement and discrepancy between reconstructed ELA and instrumental climate records
